name: Yadava-Prakasha
dates_notes: Traditional teacher of Rāmānuja prior to the latter's break; dated by relative chronology.

lineage_polemical.note: Rāmānuja famously broke from his teacher Yādava-Prakāśa; Viśiṣṭādvaita defines itself partly against this earlier bhedābheda.

core_thesis: Yādava-Prakāśa's position, reconstructed largely from Rāmānuja's *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* and *Śrī-Bhāṣya*, is a robust *bhedābheda* in which Brahman is one *substance* that genuinely undergoes triadic transformation into *cit* (sentient selves), *acit* (insentient matter), and *Īśvara* (the inner ruler). All three are real *avasthās* (states or modes) of one substance, so difference and non-difference are both ontological facts, not appearance. Crucially, on Rāmānuja's reading, Yādava treats Brahman itself — not merely a body or *upādhi* of Brahman — as undergoing the modifications that include suffering and impurity, which is precisely the consequence Rāmānuja will later refuse by introducing the *śarīra–śarīrin* relation. Yādava's *jīva* is neither illusorily nor adventitiously distinct from Brahman: it is a real *aṃśa*-like state of the one substance, its bondage and liberation being genuine alterations of that substance. This Vaiṣṇava-flavoured *bhedābheda* is the proximate target of Viśiṣṭādvaita's formation and a transitional figure between Bhāskara and Nimbārka.

engaged_works.title: Lost Bhedabheda corpus
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Original works lost; positions reconstructed from Rāmānuja's polemic, especially the Vedārtha-Saṃgraha and Śrī-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: Yādava-Prakāśa's primary writings — including, by tradition, a Brahma-Sūtra commentary and the ascetic-conduct manual *Yatidharma-Samuccaya* — survive only in fragments and in later citation. His doctrine is therefore read principally through Rāmānuja's polemic, which is sustained, detailed, and adversarial: the *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* and the opening *mahā-pūrvapakṣa* of the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* devote substantial space to refuting Yādava's view that Brahman as one substance undergoes real triadic transformation into *cit*, *acit*, and *Īśvara*. Reconstructed positions include real *pariṇāma* of Brahman into the world, the genuine ascription of suffering-states to Brahman-as-modified, and the rejection of *māyā-vāda*. The corpus's loss makes any reconstruction provisional, and the dependence on a hostile witness must be flagged at every step. The *Yatidharma-Samuccaya* ascription, though traditional, is itself sometimes contested. The figure remains pivotal as Rāmānuja's teacher and as the proximate Vaiṣṇava *bhedābheda* against which Viśiṣṭādvaita defines itself.
